content.text: Two foundational deep learning architectures shaped the modern AI landscape before transformers dominated:
, purpose-built for spatial pattern detection in images and audio, and

— including LSTM and GRU variants — designed to process ordered sequences by carrying hidden state forward through time. Understanding both is essential for any agentic AI engineer: CNNs remain the backbone of visual perception pipelines that agents use today, while RNN limitations directly explain why attention-based transformers supplanted them for language tasks.
applies a small grid of learned weights — a

or filter — across every position of an input grid (an image, a spectrogram, a 1-D signal). Each filter produces one output channel, called a feature map, that highlights where a particular pattern (an edge, a curve, a texture) appears spatially. Multiple filters run in parallel; stacking convolutional layers builds a hierarchy that represents increasingly abstract features: pixels → edges → textures → object parts → objects. Key operations include convolution, non-linear activation (ReLU), and spatial pooling (max or average) to reduce dimensionality.
processes a sequence one element at a time, maintaining a
vector that summarises everything seen so far. At each timestep t, the network receives the current input

x_t and its previous hidden state h_{t−1}, applies a learned transformation, and emits an updated hidden state h_t and optionally an output y_t. This recurrence lets the network, in principle, carry information across arbitrarily long sequences. In practice, standard RNNs suffer from vanishing gradients over long sequences;
add gating mechanisms that selectively retain or discard information, dramatically extending practical sequence lengths.

Agent engineers encounter CNNs constantly in vision pipelines: screenshot parsing, camera feeds for robotics, document layout analysis, GUI element detection, and multimodal inputs that pair images with text. Even when the final LLM uses a Vision Transformer (ViT), the design concepts — spatial locality, feature hierarchy, translation invariance — remain the same, and many production edge deployments still use CNN backbones for latency reasons.

Understanding RNN limitations is equally important conceptually. The dominant question "why did transformers replace RNNs?" has a precise technical answer rooted in RNN sequential computation, vanishing gradients, and fixed hidden-state bottlenecks. Every agent working with long document context, multi-turn dialogue history, or streaming data benefits from an engineer who understands this history. LSTM-based models still appear in time-series forecasting, low-latency signal processing, and resource-constrained environments where a 100-million-parameter transformer is unaffordable.
: A feature detector trained on a cat in the top-left corner generalises to a cat anywhere in the image because weights are shared across spatial positions.
: A 3×3 filter with 32 output channels has only 9×32 = 288 parameters — regardless of input image size — yet covers the entire spatial domain.
: RNNs handle variable-length sequences without padding to a fixed window, consuming input one element at a time in an order-preserving manner.
: At inference, an RNN maintains only the current hidden state vector, making memory usage O(1) in sequence length — critical for streaming applications.
: CNN models like MobileNet and EfficientNet-Lite run on microcontrollers. GRU models can handle real-time audio classification on edge devices where a transformer would be far too large.
: Thousands of pretrained CNN backbones on ImageNet are available for fine-tuning via transfer learning, requiring far less data than training from scratch.

: During backpropagation through time (BPTT), gradient signals must be multiplied at each timestep. Over hundreds of steps, values either collapse to zero (vanishing) or grow uncontrollably (exploding), making long-range dependency learning unreliable without LSTM/GRU gating.

: Each timestep depends on the previous hidden state, so RNN layers cannot be parallelised along the time axis. Training on a 1,024-token sequence requires 1,024 sequential steps, versus a transformer that processes all tokens simultaneously.

: The entire past is compressed into a single hidden vector of fixed dimensionality. For long documents, this information bottleneck causes the model to forget early content — a structural limit that attention sidesteps entirely.
Limited global receptive field per layer (CNNs)
: A single 3×3 conv layer has a 3×3 receptive field. Capturing long-range spatial dependencies requires many stacked layers or dilated convolutions, making deep CNNs necessary for global context.
Not designed for arbitrary discrete sequences (CNNs)

: Applying 1-D convolutions to token sequences is possible but requires large kernel sizes or multiple layers to capture long-range token dependencies — transformers do this with O(1) layers via attention.

: For language modeling beyond ~1B parameters, neither RNNs nor CNNs can match transformer scaling efficiency. The investment in training new models is better placed in transformer architectures for text.

A convolution operation slides a kernel W of shape (k_h × k_w × C_in) across the input feature map of shape (H × W × C_in), computing a dot product at each position plus bias, then applying an activation function. The output at position (i, j) for output channel d is:
Two hyperparameters control the output spatial size:
(how far the kernel moves between positions) and
(zero rows/columns added to the border so the output can match the input size). After several conv+activation pairs,
takes the maximum value in each non-overlapping k×k region, halving spatial dimensions while preserving the most active features.

is typically inserted after convolutions and before activation functions in modern CNNs. It normalises each channel across the spatial and batch dimensions, reducing internal covariate shift and enabling faster, more stable training with higher learning rates.
A vanilla RNN computes its hidden state update as:

where W_xh maps the input to hidden space and W_hh is the recurrent weight matrix. The same weight matrices are shared at every timestep (weight sharing over time), just as CNN weights are shared over space.
LSTM replaces the single hidden state with two vectors: the
c_t (long-term memory). Four gating signals control information flow:

attrs.latex: f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},\,x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(forget gate: erase old cell)}
i_t = \sigma(W_i[h_{t-1},\,x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(input gate: write new cell)}

content.text: The multiplicative gating (element-wise product ⊙) allows gradients to flow through the cell state highway with minimal decay over long sequences — resolving the vanishing gradient problem to a significant degree.
GRU simplifies LSTM by merging the forget and input gates into a single
and eliminating the separate cell state, reducing parameters by roughly one-third with comparable performance on many tasks.

The diagram below shows both architectures side-by-side: the CNN pipeline (left) builds spatial feature hierarchies from pixels to class probabilities, while the RNN/LSTM pipeline (right) propagates hidden state through a sequence of timesteps, with the LSTM cell's gating mechanism shown explicitly.

attrs.alt: CNN and RNN/LSTM architecture comparison diagram

content.text: In a CNN, each layer doubles the number of feature channels while halving spatial resolution (via pooling), producing a progressively abstract and compact representation. The final spatial features are flattened into a vector and fed to fully-connected layers for classification or regression. In an RNN, the hidden state at the final timestep (or the concatenation of all hidden states via attention) is passed to a downstream head. LSTM's cell-state conveyor belt, shown as the horizontal line at the top of the LSTM cell, is the key structural innovation enabling long-range memory.
Processing image, video frame, or spectrogram inputs where local spatial patterns are meaningful
Deploying to edge devices or embedded hardware where model size and latency constraints rule out ViT
Fine-tuning a pretrained vision backbone (MobileNet, ResNet, EfficientNet) for a downstream visual classification task
Extracting visual embeddings for a multimodal agent that pairs image features with language tokens
Detecting GUI elements or OCR bounding boxes in screen-parsing agents
Processing real-time streaming sequences where only causal (past-only) context is available and low latency matters
Deploying time-series forecasting on resource-constrained hardware (e.g., IoT sensors, microcontrollers)
Maintaining or integrating with legacy NLP systems already built on LSTM/GRU encoders
Classifying short fixed-vocabulary sequences where the overhead of a full transformer is unjustified
Building new language generation agents — use transformer decoders
Long-context document understanding tasks (sequences > 512 tokens) — RNN bottlenecks will hurt quality
Training requires GPU parallelism at scale — RNN sequential dependencies prohibit effective tensor-level parallelism
Building a CNN image classifier with PyTorch
The canonical approach is: define the feature extractor (convolutional backbone), define the classifier head (fully connected layers), combine them in a
, then train with a standard loop.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torchvision import datasets, transforms

# 1. Data pipeline with augmentation
transform_train = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
    transforms.RandomCrop(32, padding=4),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.4914, 0.4822, 0.4465),
                         (0.2470, 0.2435, 0.2616)),
])
transform_test = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.4914, 0.4822, 0.4465),
                         (0.2470, 0.2435, 0.2616)),
])

train_ds = datasets.CIFAR10(root="./data", train=True,
                            download=True, transform=transform_train)
test_ds  = datasets.CIFAR10(root="./data", train=False,
                            download=True, transform=transform_test)
train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=128, shuffle=True,  num_workers=2)
test_loader  = DataLoader(test_ds,  batch_size=256, shuffle=False, num_workers=2)

# 2. Define a small CNN
class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes: int = 10):
        super().__init__()
        self.features = nn.Sequential(
            # Block 1
            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),  # 32x32 → 32x32
            nn.BatchNorm2d(32),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Conv2d(32, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(32),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2),   # 32x32 → 16x16
            nn.Dropout2d(0.2),

            # Block 2
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1),  # 16x16
            nn.BatchNorm2d(64),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Conv2d(64, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(64),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2),   # 16x16 → 8x8
            nn.Dropout2d(0.3),
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(64 * 8 * 8, 512),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(0.5),
            nn.Linear(512, num_classes),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        x = x.flatten(1)         # flatten all spatial dims
        return self.classifier(x)

# 3. Train
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
model  = SimpleCNN().to(device)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)
scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=30)

def train_epoch(model, loader):
    model.train()
    total_loss, correct = 0.0, 0
    for images, labels in loader:
        images, labels = images.to(device), labels.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(images)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item() * images.size(0)
        correct    += (outputs.argmax(1) == labels).sum().item()
    return total_loss / len(loader.dataset), correct / len(loader.dataset)

for epoch in range(30):
    loss, acc = train_epoch(model, train_loader)
    scheduler.step()
    if (epoch + 1) % 5 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1:02d} | loss={loss:.4f} | acc={acc:.3f}")
Building an LSTM sequence classifier with PyTorch

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LSTMClassifier(nn.Module):
    """
    Binary text classifier using an LSTM over token embeddings.
    Input: token_ids of shape (batch, seq_len)
    Output: logits of shape (batch, num_classes)
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        vocab_size: int,
        embed_dim: int = 128,
        hidden_size: int = 256,
        num_layers: int = 2,
        num_classes: int = 2,
        dropout: float = 0.3,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim, padding_idx=0)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(
            input_size=embed_dim,
            hidden_size=hidden_size,
            num_layers=num_layers,
            batch_first=True,         # input/output: (batch, seq, feature)
            dropout=dropout,
            bidirectional=True,       # BiLSTM: 2x hidden_size output
        )
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size * 2, num_classes)  # *2 for BiLSTM

    def forward(self, token_ids):
        # token_ids: (batch, seq_len)
        emb = self.dropout(self.embedding(token_ids))      # (batch, seq, embed)
        out, (h_n, _) = self.lstm(emb)
        # Concatenate last forward and backward hidden states
        last_forward  = h_n[-2]   # (batch, hidden)
        last_backward = h_n[-1]   # (batch, hidden)
        combined = torch.cat([last_forward, last_backward], dim=1)
        return self.fc(self.dropout(combined))             # (batch, num_classes)


# Quick smoke test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    VOCAB, B, T = 10_000, 16, 64
    model = LSTMClassifier(vocab_size=VOCAB)
    token_ids = torch.randint(1, VOCAB, (B, T))     # batch of 16, length 64
    logits = model(token_ids)
    print(f"Input shape:  {token_ids.shape}")        # torch.Size([16, 64])
    print(f"Output shape: {logits.shape}")           # torch.Size([16, 2])
A minimal CNN on MNIST demonstrates the full pipeline from raw pixels to class probabilities without any pretrained weights.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torchvision import datasets, transforms

transform = transforms.Compose([transforms.ToTensor(),
                                transforms.Normalize((0.1307,), (0.3081,))])

train_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(
    datasets.MNIST("./data", train=True,  download=True, transform=transform),
    batch_size=64, shuffle=True)

class TinyMNISTCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(1, 16, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),   # 28×28 → 28×28×16
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),                              # 28×28 → 14×14
            nn.Conv2d(16, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),  # 14×14×32
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),                              # 14×14 → 7×7
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(32 * 7 * 7, 10),
        )
    def forward(self, x): return self.net(x)

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model  = TinyMNISTCNN().to(device)
opt    = optim.Adam(model.parameters())
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(3):
    for x, y in train_loader:
        x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
        loss = loss_fn(model(x), y)
        opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1} done")

# Count parameters
total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"Parameters: {total:,}")  # ~27,000 — extremely lightweight
Example 2: Character-level text generation with LSTM
A character-level language model is the classic RNN demonstration. Each character is encoded as an integer; the LSTM learns to predict the next character given the preceding sequence.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import random
import string

# ------ Build a tiny character vocabulary ------
CHARS  = string.ascii_lowercase + " .,!?\n"
C2I    = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(CHARS)}
I2C    = {i: c for c, i in C2I.items()}
VOCAB  = len(CHARS)

class CharLSTM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, hidden=128, layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed  = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, hidden)
        self.lstm   = nn.LSTM(hidden, hidden, layers, batch_first=True, dropout=0.2)
        self.head   = nn.Linear(hidden, vocab_size)
    def forward(self, x, state=None):
        h = self.embed(x)
        out, state = self.lstm(h, state)
        return self.head(out), state   # logits + new state

def encode(text):
    return torch.tensor([C2I.get(c, 0) for c in text.lower()], dtype=torch.long)

def sample(model, seed: str, length: int = 100, temperature: float = 0.8):
    model.eval()
    tokens = encode(seed).unsqueeze(0)   # (1, seq)
    state  = None
    result = seed
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits, state = model(tokens, state)
        for _ in range(length):
            # take last token's logits
            probs = (logits[0, -1] / temperature).softmax(-1)
            next_tok = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            result  += I2C[next_tok.item()]
            logits, state = model(next_tok.unsqueeze(0), state)
    return result

# Quick sanity check (un-trained model outputs random chars — that is expected)
model = CharLSTM(VOCAB)
print(sample(model, "the quick", length=50))
# After training on a corpus this would generate plausible text

A warehouse robotics agent combines a CNN visual front-end with an LLM reasoning backend. Cameras mounted on picking arms capture 640×480 RGB frames at 30 fps. A MobileNetV3-Small backbone (pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on warehouse product images) classifies items and detects damage in under 15 ms per frame on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano — well within the 33 ms budget at 30 fps. The backbone outputs a 960-dimensional embedding vector, which is concatenated with structured metadata (SKU, weight, shelf location) and passed as a compact context string to an LLM that decides pick-and-place actions.

The team chose a CNN backbone rather than a ViT for three reasons: (1) latency — MobileNetV3-Small runs at ~200 fps on the Jetson GPU vs ~60 fps for the smallest ViT at equivalent accuracy; (2) model size — MobileNetV3-Small is 5.5 MB vs 85 MB for ViT-S/16; (3) data efficiency — convolutional inductive biases require far fewer labelled examples to fine-tune than ViT's attention layers, which need more data to learn spatial structure from scratch.

import torch
import torchvision.models as models
import torchvision.transforms as T
from PIL import Image

# Load pretrained MobileNetV3-Small, replace head for 5-class product classifier
backbone = models.mobilenet_v3_small(weights=models.MobileNet_V3_Small_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1)
backbone.classifier[3] = torch.nn.Linear(1024, 5)  # 5 product categories

backbone.eval()

preprocess = T.Compose([
    T.Resize((224, 224)),
    T.ToTensor(),
    T.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
])

def classify_item(image_path: str) -> dict:
    img    = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")
    tensor = preprocess(img).unsqueeze(0)
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = backbone(tensor)
    probs  = logits.softmax(1).squeeze()
    class_id = probs.argmax().item()
    return {"class_id": class_id, "confidence": probs[class_id].item()}

# In the agent loop this runs every frame; the result feeds the LLM context.
: Vanilla RNNs almost never converge on sequences longer than ~20 tokens due to vanishing gradients. Always start with LSTM or GRU.
Forgetting to reset hidden state between unrelated batches
: If training on unrelated text segments packed into batches, carrying hidden state across segments leaks information. Reset state at sequence boundaries.
Using RNN for new long-context language agent pipelines
: For dialogue history, RAG context, or multi-document understanding, transformers with attention are the correct choice.
Not using bidirectional LSTM for classification
: For tasks that see the whole sequence (sentiment analysis, NER, document classification), bidirectional LSTMs read context from both directions and consistently outperform unidirectional models.
Training from scratch on small datasets
: A CNN with random initialisation needs hundreds of thousands of images to learn useful features. Always fine-tune a pretrained backbone for domain-specific tasks.
: Without BatchNorm, deeper CNNs struggle to train. Include BatchNorm after each conv layer, especially in networks deeper than ~4 layers.
Not normalising input to pretrained backbone's training statistics
: Using ImageNet-pretrained weights requires inputs normalised to ImageNet mean/std; feeding raw [0,1] pixels degrades accuracy measurably.
Applying global average pooling too early
: Inserting global average pooling before spatial resolution reaches a small size (e.g., 7×7) discards fine-grained spatial information prematurely.
Wrong vs. right — LSTM hidden state reset:

# WRONG: hidden state bleeds across unrelated sequences
model = LSTMClassifier(vocab_size=10_000)
state = None   # never reset

for batch_tokens, labels in dataloader:
    logits, state = model(batch_tokens)   # state leaks!
    loss = loss_fn(logits, labels)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

# RIGHT: detach or reset state between independent sequences
state = None

for batch_tokens, labels in dataloader:
    logits, state = model(batch_tokens)
    state = (state[0].detach(), state[1].detach())  # cut gradient graph; keep value
    # OR: state = None  # full reset if batches are unrelated
    loss = loss_fn(logits, labels)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
Always start from a pretrained backbone
for vision tasks. Freeze early layers, unfreeze progressively during fine-tuning. This reduces data requirements by 10-100×.
: Random horizontal flips, rotations, color jitter, and cutout augmentation are standard for image classification and dramatically reduce overfitting.
Use bidirectional LSTM for offline sequence tasks
(classification, NER, summarization where full input is available). Reserve unidirectional LSTM for causal/streaming tasks.
: Clip gradient norms to 1.0–5.0 to prevent exploding gradients even in LSTM, which reduces vanishing gradients but not explosions.
or ONNX Runtime profiling before committing to an architecture for a production agent. CNN vs. ViT vs. LSTM latency differences can be 5-10× at practical batch sizes.
Export to ONNX or TensorRT for edge deployment
: Both CNN and RNN models export cleanly to ONNX; TensorRT on Jetson devices yields 3–5× additional speedup over PyTorch eager mode.
) consistently outperforms fixed LR for both CNN and LSTM training. Start at 1e-3, decay to 1e-5.
A single convolutional layer with C_in input channels, C_out output channels, kernel size k, and input spatial size H×W has:

Parameter count for the layer is only k² × C_in × C_out + C_out (bias). For a 3×3 conv with 64 input and 128 output channels: 9 × 64 × 128 = 73,728 weights. The same output for a fully-connected layer mapping 64×H×W → 128×H×W would require orders of magnitude more parameters, demonstrating CNN efficiency.

Modern CNN backbones scale well: MobileNetV3-Small achieves 67.4% top-1 on ImageNet with 5.5M parameters and 0.06 GFLOPs per forward pass. ResNet-50 achieves 76.1% with 25M parameters and 4.1 GFLOPs. EfficientNet-B7 achieves 84.3% with 66M parameters. Scaling follows predictable efficiency-vs-accuracy curves governed by compound scaling of depth, width, and resolution.

RNN inference is O(T) in time and O(1) in memory per step (constant hidden state size), making it ideal for streaming inference. Training via backpropagation through time (BPTT) is O(T) in both time and memory, which is prohibitive for very long sequences. Truncated BPTT (processing K-step windows with detached hidden states) limits memory to O(K), enabling long-sequence training.
Benchmark comparison on a single A100 GPU (batch=32, sequence length=512):

The CNN's throughput advantage for short sequences comes entirely from parallelism. The transformer's scaling advantage for longer sequences comes from the attention mechanism allowing all-pair interactions in a single layer. RNN throughput is fundamentally bounded by sequential computation.
library of pretrained CNN backbones. It is the dominant framework for research and most production agent workflows.
provide equivalent functionality. Keras's higher-level API is easier for rapid prototyping; TF-Serving enables scalable model deployment.

# Export PyTorch CNN to ONNX, then run with ONNXRuntime
import torch
import onnx
import onnxruntime as ort
import numpy as np

model  = SimpleCNN().eval()
dummy  = torch.zeros(1, 3, 32, 32)

torch.onnx.export(
    model, dummy, "model.onnx",
    input_names=["image"], output_names=["logits"],
    dynamic_axes={"image": {0: "batch_size"}},
    opset_version=17,
)

# Verify
onnx.checker.check_model("model.onnx")

# Run inference
sess = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx",
       providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider", "CPUExecutionProvider"])
output = sess.run(["logits"], {"image": np.zeros((1,3,32,32), dtype=np.float32)})
print(output[0].shape)   # (1, 10)
: Library of 700+ pretrained vision models (CNNs, ViTs, hybrid). Single line to load a EfficientNet or ResNet with pretrained weights.
: Provides LSTM-based models for legacy NLP tasks; also hosts CNN-based vision models.
: NVIDIA's inference optimizer that fuses and quantizes CNN/LSTM graphs, achieving 3–5× speedup on Jetson and A100 hardware.
: Mobile inference runtimes that accept ONNX-exported CNN models for on-device deployment on Android and iOS.
Streaming signal, legacy NLP, edge time-series

The most important takeaway is that CNN and Transformer/ViT are complementary rather than competitors for vision tasks: many production multimodal models use a CNN or ViT as the visual feature extractor, then a transformer decoder for language generation. RNN and Transformer are genuinely competing sequence models for language — transformers win at scale, RNNs win on edge hardware and streaming requirements.

CNNs are vulnerable to adversarial examples: imperceptible pixel perturbations (e.g., FGSM — Fast Gradient Sign Method) can cause a CNN to misclassify an image with high confidence. In agent settings where CNNs parse screenshots or document images, adversarial manipulation is a realistic attack surface if inputs arrive from untrusted sources.
: Augment training data with adversarial examples to improve robustness at the cost of some clean accuracy.
: JPEG compression, random smoothing, or feature squeezing as preprocessing can disrupt many white-box attacks.
: Reject predictions where softmax confidence is below 0.8 — adversarial examples often produce low or contradictory confidence distributions.

: CNN accuracy degrades when test images differ from training distribution (e.g., new camera angles, different lighting). Deploy statistical monitoring (e.g., MMD tests on activation distributions) to detect distribution shift.
: INT8 quantization for edge CNN deployment can silently degrade accuracy on minority classes. Always evaluate quantized models on a held-out validation set before deployment.

: Without gradient clipping during training, LSTM weights can produce NaN or Inf hidden states at inference when input sequences are longer than those seen during training. Clip activations at inference time as a safety measure.
: Store model weights in versioned artifact stores (MLflow, DVC). Never overwrite production weights without a rollback path.
Think of a CNN as a team of

. Each spotter carries a small template (the kernel) and slides it across every patch of the image. At each position the spotter shouts "match!" or "no match" depending on how well the local patch resembles the template. The next team of spotters works on the first team's output, looking for patterns-of-patterns. By the time input reaches the final layer, the spotters have collectively assembled a concept ("this is a cat") from pixels.

Think of an RNN as a reader with a single sticky note. The reader reads one word at a time from a long document, updating the sticky note with a compressed summary after each word. When finished, the sticky note holds the model's entire "memory" of the text. LSTM gives the reader a second, larger sticky note with special highlighter pens — a forget pen to erase outdated information, an input pen to add new highlights, and an output pen to expose only what's currently relevant. This two-note system allows important early facts ("the detective found the glove") to persist across hundreds of intervening words.

Transformers discard the sticky-note metaphor entirely: every word can look at every other word simultaneously, like a conference room discussion instead of a one-person relay race. That is why transformers parallelise well, why they scale to trillion-parameter models, and why sequential RNNs were replaced — not because RNNs were wrong, but because the conference-room architecture matched GPU hardware and large-data regimes far better.
CNNs detect local spatial patterns via weight-shared convolution
— the same kernel scans every position, yielding translation invariance and massive parameter efficiency compared to fully-connected layers.
RNNs process sequences step-by-step with a hidden state
, but vanilla RNNs fail on long sequences due to vanishing gradients; LSTM and GRU gating mechanisms extend practical memory significantly.
The fundamental RNN bottleneck is sequential computation
— no timestep can be processed until the previous one completes, preventing the GPU parallelism that transformers exploit.
Transformers replaced RNNs for language modeling
because self-attention allows all tokens to interact in one layer, with no sequential bottleneck and no fixed-size memory ceiling.
CNNs remain the dominant vision backbone in production agents
where latency, model size, and data efficiency matter — particularly MobileNet and EfficientNet variants on edge hardware.
Always fine-tune a pretrained CNN backbone
rather than training from scratch; ImageNet-pretrained features transfer broadly and reduce labelled-data requirements by 10-100×.
Use gradient clipping (norm ≤ 1.0–5.0) when training LSTMs
— LSTM gating removes vanishing gradients but not exploding gradients; unclipped training can destabilise weights.
For new agent projects, CNNs serve vision and RNNs serve edge streaming
; transformers serve language and multimodal reasoning — combining architectures for each modality outperforms applying one architecture everywhere.
